<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead">
You are working on beads issue kapow-raf: CI workflow gate.

## Issue Details
The repo has local Make-based quality targets, but there is no checked-in CI workflow enforcing them on every change set. Quality currently depends too much on developers remembering to run the right commands locally.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A checked-in CI workflow runs the standard quality gate on every merge path
- Failures block merges
- The workflow is documented in README or contributing docs
- The CI gate uses repo-supported commands rather than ad hoc scripts

## Task
Your task ID is #5 in the team task list. Claim it with TaskUpdate (owner: "ci-workflow", status: "in_progress") before starting.

## Quality Gate
Since this touches config/docs, just make sure the YAML is valid and the README update is correct. No code quality gate needed per se, but verify the workflow file syntax.

## WORKTREE ISOLATION â€” CRITICAL

### Create your worktree (MANDATORY FIRST STEP)
Before doing ANY other work, create and enter your worktree:

```bash
WORKTREE_NAME="ci-workflow"
REPO_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
WORKTREE_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/.claude/worktrees/$WORKTREE_NAME"
BRANCH_NAME="worktree/$WORKTREE_NAME"

if [ -d "$WORKTREE_DIR" ]; then
  echo "Worktree already exists, reusing"
else
  git worktree add "$WORKTREE_DIR" -b "$BRANCH_NAME"
fi

cd "$WORKTREE_DIR"
```

Then verify:

```bash
echo "=== WORKTREE VERIFICATION ===" && \
echo "CWD: $(pwd)" && \
echo "Branch: $(git branch --show-current)" && \
if echo "$(pwd)" | grep -q '\.claude/worktrees/'; then \
  echo "âś“ WORKTREE VERIFIED"; \
else \
  echo "âś— FATAL: NOT in a worktree! CWD=$(pwd)"; \
  exit 1; \
fi
```

### Rules
1. NEVER cd to the main repo â€” stay in your worktree directory
2. NEVER run `git checkout` to switch branches
3. ALWAYS use relative paths or `$(pwd)` for all operations
4. Before EVERY `git commit` and `git push`, re-verify your CWD is in `.claude/worktrees/`
5. Commit and push ONLY on your worktree branch â€” never touch main
6. When running `make` targets, run them from your worktree root
7. If ANY command output shows your CWD changed to the main repo, STOP

### How to push your branch
```bash
git add <files>
git commit -m "your message"
git push origin HEAD
```
NEVER run `git push origin main`.

## Instructions
- The repo uses GitHub Actions (check if .github/workflows/ exists, create if not)
- The standard quality gate is `make test-standard` which runs: Go unit tests + lint + TS build + ESLint + web tests
- The workflow should trigger on push to main and on pull requests
- Follow CLAUDE.md conventions
- Push your branch before reporting completion
- Mark task #5 as completed when done via TaskUpdate
- Send a message to the team lead when finished

</teammate-message>

1â†’# Kapow â€” College Power Search
     2â†’
     3â†’A college/university search platform with a Go API backend and React frontend.
     4â†’
     5â†’## Quick Start with Docker (recommended)
     6â†’
     7â†’The fastest way to try Kapow â€” a single Docker image bundles PostgreSQL, the API
     8â†’server, and the web frontend with pre-loaded fixture data.
     9â†’
    10â†’```bash
    11â†’# Build the all-in-one image
    12â†’make test-image-build
    13â†’
    14â†’# Run it (exposes port 8080)
    15â†’make test-image-run
    16â†’```
    17â†’
    18â†’Open **http://localhost:8080** in your browser. To run the interactive demo
    19â†’against it, see [DEMO.md](DEMO.md).
    20â†’
    21â†’The container includes:
    22â†’
    23â†’- PostgreSQL 15 + PostGIS with pre-loaded college data
    24â†’- Go API server on internal port 8081
    25â†’- nginx reverse proxy serving the React frontend and proxying API requests
    26â†’
    27â†’No configuration needed â€” the container handles everything on first boot,
    28â†’including database initialization and fixture data loading.
    29â†’
    30â†’To stop: press `Ctrl+C`.
    31â†’
    32â†’---
    33â†’
    34â†’## Devcontainer
    35â†’
    36â†’See [.devcontainer/README.md](.devcontainer/README.md) for setup instructions
    37â†’(GitHub Codespaces, VS Code, and CLI).
    38â†’
    39â†’---
    40â†’
    41â†’## Local Development Setup
    42â†’
    43â†’### Prerequisites
    44â†’
    45â†’- **Go** 1.23+
    46â†’- **Node.js** 22+ with **pnpm** 9+ (`npm install -g pnpm`)
    47â†’- **Docker** (for the dev database container)
    48â†’- **psql** (PostgreSQL client) â€” `brew install libpq` on macOS, `apt install postgresql-client` on Linux
    49â†’
    50â†’Optional:
    51â†’
    52â†’- **golangci-lint** for linting
    53â†’- **air** for standalone API hot-reload: `go install github.com/air-verse/air@latest` (`make dev` uses `go run` instead, no install needed)
    54â†’
    55â†’### Quick start (recommended)
    56â†’
    57â†’A single command starts everything â€” PostgreSQL in Docker, the Go API with hot
    58â†’reload, and the Vite dev server:
    59â†’
    60â†’```bash
    61â†’make dev
    62â†’```
    63â†’
    64â†’This will:
    65â†’- Start a PostgreSQL + PostGIS container on port **15432** (won't conflict with a host PostgreSQL)
    66â†’- Load fixture data on first run (subsequent runs skip this step)
    67â†’- Run database migrations
    68â†’- Start the API with **air** (hot reload) on **:8081**
    69â†’- Start the web dev server on **:8080** with HMR
    70â†’
    71â†’Open **http://localhost:8080** and you're ready to go. Press **Ctrl+C** to stop
    72â†’everything. The database volume persists between runs, so the next `make dev`
    73â†’starts much faster.
    74â†’
    75â†’```bash
    76â†’make dev-clean    # remove this checkout's DB volume (forces fresh fixture load)
    77â†’make dev-prune    # remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days
    78â†’```
    79â†’
    80â†’### Manual setup (alternative)
    81â†’
    82â†’If you prefer to manage PostgreSQL yourself:
    83â†’
    84â†’#### 1. Configure environment
    85â†’
    86â†’```bash
    87â†’cp .env.example .env
    88â†’```
    89â†’
    90â†’Edit `.env` and set at minimum:
    91â†’
    92â†’```
    93â†’DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:password@localhost:5432/kapow?sslmode=disable
    94â†’JWT_SECRET=change-me-to-a-long-random-secret
    95â†’```
    96â†’
    97â†’#### 2. Set up the database
    98â†’
    99â†’Create the database, then run migrations:
   100â†’
   101â†’```bash
   102â†’createdb kapow
   103â†’make api-migrate-up
   104â†’```
   105â†’
   106â†’To load fixture data (from `common/src/main/resources/data/`):
   107â†’
   108â†’```bash
   109â†’xz -dc common/src/main/resources/data/complete.sql.xz | psql kapow
   110â†’```
   111â†’
   112â†’Smaller subsets are available for faster loading:
   113â†’
   114â†’- `subset-VT.sql.xz` â€” Vermont only
   115â†’- `subset-DE_MA_VT.sql.xz` â€” Delaware, Massachusetts, Vermont
   116â†’
   117â†’#### 3. Start the API
   118â†’
   119â†’```bash
   120â†’make api-run
   121â†’```
   122â†’
   123â†’The API listens on **localhost:8081**. Verify with:
   124â†’
   125â†’```bash
   126â†’curl http://localhost:8081/private/healthcheck | jq .
   127â†’```
   128â†’
   129â†’#### 4. Start the web frontend
   130â†’
   131â†’In a separate terminal:
   132â†’
   133â†’```bash
   134â†’make web-install   # first time only
   135â†’make web-dev
   136â†’```
   137â†’
   138â†’The dev server runs on **localhost:8080** and proxies `/graphql` and `/api`
   139â†’requests to the API at :8081.
   140â†’
   141â†’Open **http://localhost:8080**.
   142â†’
   143â†’#### 5. Authentication with Supabase (optional)
   144â†’
   145â†’The GraphQL endpoint (`/graphql`) requires a valid JWT. In development you can
   146â†’use any HS256 secret via `JWT_SECRET`. For production (or to test real login
   147â†’flows), connect a Supabase project.
   148â†’
   149â†’##### Create a Supabase project
   150â†’
   151â†’1. Go to [supabase.com/dashboard](https://supabase.com/dashboard) and create a
   152â†’   new project (or use an existing one).
   153â†’2. Note your **Project URL** (`https://<project-ref>.supabase.co`) and
   154â†’   **anon public** key from **Settings â†’ API**.
   155â†’
   156â†’##### Configure API environment variables
   157â†’
   158â†’In `.env`, set the JWT vars so the Go API can validate Supabase-issued tokens:
   159â†’
   160â†’```
   161â†’JWT_SECRET=<your-supabase-jwt-secret>
   162â†’JWT_ISSUER=https://<project-ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1
   163â†’JWT_AUDIENCE=authenticated
   164â†’```
   165â†’
   166â†’The JWT Secret is on **Settings â†’ API â†’ JWT Secret**. Alternatively, paste the
   167â†’**JWT Public Key** (RS256) into `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` for asymmetric validation.
   168â†’
   169â†’##### Configure frontend environment variables
   170â†’
   171â†’The web frontend uses the Supabase JS SDK for login/signup. Add these to `.env`:
   172â†’
   173â†’```
   174â†’VITE_SUPABASE_URL=https://<project-ref>.supabase.co
   175â†’VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=<your-anon-key>
   176â†’```
   177â†’
   178â†’The `VITE_` prefix is required so Vite exposes them to browser code.
   179â†’
   180â†’##### Enable OAuth providers
   181â†’
   182â†’To support social login (Google, Apple, etc.):
   183â†’
   184â†’1. In the Supabase dashboard, go to **Authentication â†’ Providers**.
   185â†’2. **Google**: Enable the Google provider, paste your Google OAuth **Client ID**
   186â†’   and **Client Secret** (from [Google Cloud Console â†’ Credentials](https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials)),
   187â†’   and add your Supabase callback URL as an authorized redirect URI in Google.
   188â†’3. **Apple**: Enable the Apple provider, upload your **Services ID**, **Team ID**,
   189â†’   **Key ID**, and **private key** (from
   190â†’   [Apple Developer â†’ Certificates, Identifiers & Profiles](https://developer.apple.com/account/resources)).
   191â†’   Add the Supabase callback URL to your Apple Sign In configuration.
   192â†’
   193â†’Each provider's callback URL is shown in the Supabase dashboard when you enable
   194â†’it (typically `https://<project-ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1/callback`).
   195â†’
   196â†’##### How it works
   197â†’
   198â†’On the first authenticated request, the API automatically creates a personal
   199â†’team and membership for the user (using the JWT `sub` claim as the user ID).
   200â†’

   202â†’
   203â†’## Project Structure
   204â†’
   205â†’```
   206â†’kapow/
   207â†’â”śâ”€â”€ api/          # Go API server (GraphQL, search engine, auth)
   208â†’â”śâ”€â”€ web/          # React + TypeScript + Vite frontend
   209â†’â”śâ”€â”€ common/       # Shared resources (generated schema, fixture data)
   210â†’â”śâ”€â”€ docker/       # Container config (nginx, supervisord, entrypoint)
   211â†’â”śâ”€â”€ scripts/      # Shell utilities (smoke tests, schema generation)
   212â†’â”śâ”€â”€ tools/        # Developer utilities (see below)
   213â†’â”śâ”€â”€ Dockerfile    # All-in-one multi-stage build
   214â†’â””â”€â”€ Makefile      # Root â€” delegates to api/ and web/
   215â†’```
   216â†’
   217â†’### tools/
   218â†’
   219â†’Developer utilities that operate on the database or fixture data.
   220â†’
   221â†’| Tool | Description |
   222â†’|---|---|
   223â†’| `tools/kapow` | Data pipeline â€” fetches, caches, and imports data from government and third-party sources (NCES, etc.) into the Kapow PostgreSQL database. |
   224â†’| `tools/samplesql` | Deterministic institution sampler â€” connects to a live database, randomly selects N institutions (default 100, seed 42), extracts all dependent rows, and writes a self-contained SQL fixture file using `COPY` statements. Useful for generating small, reproducible test datasets. |
   225â†’
   226â†’**kapow usage:**
   227â†’
   228â†’```bash
   229â†’cd tools/kapow
   230â†’go run . --help              # show usage
   231â†’go run . import nces         # import NCES data
   232â†’go run . run-all             # fetch all + import all in dependency order
   233â†’```
   234â†’
   235â†’Make targets: `make kapow-build`, `make kapow-test`, `make kapow-test-unit`.
   236â†’
   237â†’**samplesql usage:**
   238â†’
   239â†’```bash
   240â†’cd tools/samplesql
   241â†’go run . --database-url "$DATABASE_URL" --limit 100 --seed 42 --output sample.sql
   242â†’```
   243â†’
   244â†’Flags: `--database-url` (or `DATABASE_URL` env var), `--limit` (number of institutions), `--seed` (deterministic random seed), `--output` (file path; defaults to stdout).

1â†’#!/usr/bin/env bash
     2â†’# scripts/parallel-run.sh â€” Run make targets in parallel with clean output
     3â†’# Usage: scripts/parallel-run.sh target1 target2 target3 ...
     4â†’#
     5â†’# Each target runs as a background make invocation. Output is captured per-target
     6â†’# and printed sequentially when all jobs finish, so logs never interleave.
     7â†’# Exits non-zero if any target fails.
     8â†’set -uo pipefail
     9â†’
    10â†’if [ $# -eq 0 ]; then
    11â†’    echo "Usage: $0 target1 [target2 ...]" >&2
    12â†’    exit 1
    13â†’fi
    14â†’
    15â†’# Colors
    16â†’GREEN=$'\033[0;32m'
    17â†’RED=$'\033[0;31m'
    18â†’BLUE=$'\033[0;34m'
    19â†’BOLD=$'\033[1m'
    20â†’NC=$'\033[0m'
    21â†’
    22â†’TMPDIR_RUN=$(mktemp -d)
    23â†’PIDS=()
    24â†’TARGETS=("$@")
    25â†’
    26â†’cleanup() {
    27â†’    for pid in "${PIDS[@]}"; do
    28â†’        kill "$pid" 2>/dev/null || true
    29â†’    done
    30â†’    rm -rf "$TMPDIR_RUN"
    31â†’}
    32â†’trap cleanup EXIT
    33â†’
    34â†’# Launch all targets in background
    35â†’for target in "${TARGETS[@]}"; do
    36â†’    logfile="${TMPDIR_RUN}/${target}.log"
    37â†’    ${MAKE:-make} "$target" > "$logfile" 2>&1 &
    38â†’    PIDS+=($!)
    39â†’done
    40â†’
    41â†’# Wait for each and collect exit codes
    42â†’CODES=()
    43â†’for i in "${!PIDS[@]}"; do
    44â†’    wait "${PIDS[$i]}" 2>/dev/null
    45â†’    CODES+=($?)
    46â†’done
    47â†’
    48â†’# Print results
    49â†’FAILED=0
    50â†’echo ""
    51â†’echo "${BOLD}â”€â”€ Parallel results â”€â”€${NC}"
    52â†’for i in "${!TARGETS[@]}"; do
    53â†’    target="${TARGETS[$i]}"
    54â†’    code="${CODES[$i]}"
    55â†’    logfile="${TMPDIR_RUN}/${target}.log"
    56â†’    if [ "$code" -eq 0 ]; then
    57â†’        echo "${GREEN}PASS${NC} ${target}"
    58â†’    else
    59â†’        echo "${RED}FAIL${NC} ${target} (exit ${code})"
    60â†’        FAILED=1
    61â†’    fi
    62â†’done
    63â†’echo ""
    64â†’
    65â†’# Print output for failed targets (so you can see what went wrong)
    66â†’for i in "${!TARGETS[@]}"; do
    67â†’    target="${TARGETS[$i]}"
    68â†’    code="${CODES[$i]}"
    69â†’    logfile="${TMPDIR_RUN}/${target}.log"
    70â†’    if [ "$code" -ne 0 ]; then
    71â†’        echo "${RED}${BOLD}â”€â”€ Output: ${target} â”€â”€${NC}"
    72â†’        cat "$logfile"
    73â†’        echo ""
    74â†’    fi
    75â†’done
    76â†’
    77â†’# On verbose or if all passed, optionally show output
    78â†’# To see all output even on success, set PARALLEL_VERBOSE=1
    79â†’if [ "${PARALLEL_VERBOSE:-0}" = "1" ]; then
    80â†’    for i in "${!TARGETS[@]}"; do
    81â†’        target="${TARGETS[$i]}"
    82â†’        code="${CODES[$i]}"
    83â†’        logfile="${TMPDIR_RUN}/${target}.log"
    84â†’        if [ "$code" -eq 0 ]; then
    85â†’            echo "${BLUE}${BOLD}â”€â”€ Output: ${target} â”€â”€${NC}"
    86â†’            cat "$logfile"
    87â†’            echo ""
    88â†’        fi
    89â†’    done
    90â†’fi
    91â†’
    92â†’exit "$FAILED"
    93â†’

1â†’#!/usr/bin/env bash
     2â†’# test-fixture.sh â€” Validate that fixture data loads cleanly against current schema.
     3â†’#
     4â†’# Spins up a temporary PostGIS container, runs init-db.sh (migrations + fixture),
     5â†’# then checks that expected tables have non-zero row counts.
     6â†’#
     7â†’# Usage:
     8â†’#   ./scripts/test-fixture.sh
     9â†’#
    10â†’# Exit codes:
    11â†’#   0  All validations passed
    12â†’#   1  Fixture load or validation failed
    13â†’
    14â†’set -euo pipefail
    15â†’
    16â†’REPO="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
    17â†’
    18â†’# â”€â”€ Color helpers â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
    19â†’GREEN=$'\033[0;32m'
    20â†’RED=$'\033[0;31m'
    21â†’YELLOW=$'\033[1;33m'
    22â†’NC=$'\033[0m'
    23â†’
    24â†’log() { printf '%s\n' "${YELLOW}[test-fixture]${NC} $*"; }
    25â†’
    26â†’# â”€â”€ Dependency checks â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
    27â†’for cmd in docker psql pg_isready; do
    28â†’  if ! command -v "$cmd" &>/dev/null; then
    29â†’    echo "${RED}error:${NC} $cmd is required but not found" >&2
    30â†’    exit 1
    31â†’  fi
    32â†’done
    33â†’
    34â†’# â”€â”€ Container config â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
    35â†’CONTAINER_NAME="kapow-fixture-test-$$"
    36â†’VOLUME_NAME="kapow-fixture-test-$$"
    37â†’PORT=$((30000 + RANDOM % 30000))
    38â†’DB_USER="postgres"
    39â†’DB_NAME="kapow_fixture_test"
    40â†’
    41â†’# â”€â”€ Cleanup trap â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
    42â†’cleanup() {
    43â†’  local exit_code=$?
    44â†’  log "Cleaning up..."
    45â†’  docker rm -f "$CONTAINER_NAME" 2>/dev/null || true
    46â†’  docker volume rm "$VOLUME_NAME" 2>/dev/null || true
    47â†’  exit $exit_code
    48â†’}
    49â†’trap cleanup EXIT INT TERM
    50â†’
    51â†’# â”€â”€ Start PostGIS container â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
    52â†’log "Starting PostGIS container on port $PORT..."
    53â†’docker run -d \
    54â†’  --name "$CONTAINER_NAME" \
    55â†’  -v "$VOLUME_NAME:/var/lib/postgresql/data" \
    56â†’  -e POSTGRES_USER="$DB_USER" \
    57â†’  -e POSTGRES_DB="$DB_NAME" \
    58â†’  -e POSTGRES_HOST_AUTH_METHOD=trust \
    59â†’  -p "$PORT:5432" \
    60â†’  postgis/postgis:15-3.4 \
    61â†’  >/dev/null
    62â†’
    63â†’export DATABASE_URL="postgres://${DB_USER}@localhost:${PORT}/${DB_NAME}"
    64â†’
    65â†’# â”€â”€ Wait for PostgreSQL â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
    66â†’log "Waiting for PostgreSQL..."
    67â†’for i in $(seq 1 60); do
    68â†’  if pg_isready -h localhost -p "$PORT" -U "$DB_USER" &>/dev/null; then
    69â†’    break
    70â†’  fi

## Kapow API Makefile
## â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€â”€
## Usage: make <target>
## Targets requiring DATABASE_URL: migrate-up, migrate-down, migrate-status, migrate-create

BINARY      := bin/kapow-api
CMD         := ./cmd/server
MODULE      := github.com/ketang/kapow/api
VERSION     := $(shell git describe --tags --always --dirty 2>/dev/null || echo dev)
BUILD_TIME  := $(shell date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)
LDFLAGS     := -ldflags "-X $(MODULE)/internal/version.Version=$(VERSION) \
                          -X $(MODULE)/internal/version.BuildTime=$(BUILD_TIME)"

GOLANGCI_LINT := $(shell which golangci-lint 2>/dev/null || echo $(shell go env GOPATH)/bin/golangci-lint)

GOOSE_DRIVER  := postgres
GOOSE_DBSTRING ?= $(DATABASE_URL)
GOOSE_DIR     := migrations

.PHONY: build build-full run genenum generate lint vet fmt tidy clean \
        migrate-up migrate-down migrate-status migrate-create \
        test test-unit test-integration dev schema-gen help

## build: Compile the server binary into bin/kapow-api
build:
	go build $(LDFLAGS) -o $(BINARY) $(CMD)

## build-full: Build with embedded web frontend (requires web/dist/ to exist)
build-full:
	rm -rf ui/dist
	cp -r ../web/dist ui/dist
	go build $(LDFLAGS) -tags ui -o $(BINARY) $(CMD)
	rm -rf ui/dist

## run: Run the server directly (no build step)
run:
	go run $(LDFLAGS) $(CMD)

## genenum: Generate SearchFieldName enum from registry
genenum:
	go run ./cmd/genenum

## generate: Re-run gqlgen code generation (runs genenum first)
generate: genenum
	go generate ./...

## lint: Run go vet + golangci-lint (includes gosec security checks)
lint:
	go vet ./...
	$(GOLANGCI_LINT) run ./...

## vet: Run go vet
vet:
	go vet ./...

## fmt: Format all Go source files
fmt:
	gofmt -w .

## tidy: Tidy go.mod and go.sum
tidy:
	go mod tidy

## clean: Remove build artifacts
clean:
	rm -rf bin/

## migrate-up: Apply all pending database migrations
migrate-up:
	DATABASE_URL=$(GOOSE_DBSTRING) go run github.com/pressly/goose/v3/cmd/goose \
		-dir $(GOOSE_DIR) $(GOOSE_DRIVER) "$(GOOSE_DBSTRING)" up

## migrate-down: Roll back the most recent migration
migrate-down:
	DATABASE_URL=$(GOOSE_DBSTRING) go run github.com/pressly/goose/v3/cmd/goose \
		-dir $(GOOSE_DIR) $(GOOSE_DRIVER) "$(GOOSE_DBSTRING)" down

## migrate-status: Show migration status
migrate-status:
	go run github.com/pressly/goose/v3/cmd/goose \
		-dir $(GOOSE_DIR) $(GOOSE_DRIVER) "$(GOOSE_DBSTRING)" status

## migrate-create NAME=<name>: Create a new migration file
migrate-create:
	go run github.com/pressly/goose/v3/cmd/goose \
		-dir $(GOOSE_DIR) $(GOOSE_DRIVER) "$(GOOSE_DBSTRING)" create $(NAME) sql

## test: Run all tests with race detector
test:
	go test -race ./...

## test-unit: Run unit tests only (short mode)
test-unit:
	go test -race -short ./...

## test-integration: Run integration tests (starts a container automatically; set TEST_DATABASE_URL to use an existing DB instead)
test-integration:
	go test -race -run Integration ./...

## dev: Run with air hot-reload (requires: go install github.com/air-verse/air@latest)

## Kapow Web â€” pnpm + Vite frontend

.PHONY: install dev build lint format clean test test-coverage e2e-install e2e-test e2e-test-critical screenshots storybook

## install: Install dependencies
install:
	pnpm install

## dev: Start Vite dev server (port 8080)
dev:
	pnpm dev

## build: Type-check and build for production
build:
	pnpm build

## lint: Run ESLint
lint:
	pnpm lint

## format: Run Prettier
format:
	pnpm format

## preview: Preview production build locally
preview:
	pnpm preview

## test: Run unit tests
test:
	pnpm test

## test-coverage: Run unit tests with coverage report
test-coverage:
	pnpm test:coverage

## e2e-install: Install Playwright browser binaries (run once after pnpm install)
e2e-install:
	pnpm exec playwright install chromium

## e2e-test: Run Playwright end-to-end tests (starts Vite dev server automatically)
e2e-test:
	pnpm test:e2e

## e2e-test-critical: Run only @critical tier E2E tests
e2e-test-critical:
	pnpm test:e2e --grep @critical

## screenshots: Capture visual baseline screenshots across viewports
screenshots:
	mkdir -p e2e/screenshots
	pnpm exec playwright test e2e/screenshots.spec.ts

## storybook: Start Storybook dev server (port 6006)
storybook:
	pnpm storybook

## clean: Remove build artifacts and node_modules
clean:
	rm -rf dist node_modules .pnpm-store

# Kapow â€” College Power Search

A college/university search platform with a Go API backend and React frontend.

## Quick Start with Docker (recommended)

The fastest way to try Kapow â€” a single Docker image bundles PostgreSQL, the API
server, and the web frontend with pre-loaded fixture data.

```bash
# Build the all-in-one image
make test-image-build

# Run it (exposes port 8080)
make test-image-run
```

Open **http://localhost:8080** in your browser. To run the interactive demo
against it, see [DEMO.md](DEMO.md).

The container includes:

- PostgreSQL 15 + PostGIS with pre-loaded college data
- Go API server on internal port 8081
- nginx reverse proxy serving the React frontend and proxying API requests

No configuration needed â€” the container handles everything on first boot,
including database initialization and fixture data loading.

To stop: press `Ctrl+C`.

# CI Workflow Gate â€” Implementation Plan

## Context

The repo has well-structured Make-based quality tiers (`test-quick`, `test-standard`, `test-full`) but no CI workflow enforcing them. Quality depends on developers remembering to run checks locally. This plan adds a GitHub Actions workflow that runs `make test-standard` on every push/PR, blocking merges on failure.

## Files to Create/Modify

1. **Create** `.github/workflows/ci.yml` â€” GitHub Actions workflow
2. **Edit** `README.md` â€” Add CI section documenting the workflow

## Workflow Design: `.github/workflows/ci.yml`

**Trigger**: `push` to `main` + all `pull_request` events

**Environment**: Ubuntu latest with Go 1.23, Node 22, pnpm 9

**Steps**:
1. Checkout code
2. Set up Go 1.23 with module caching
3. Set up Node 22 + pnpm 9 with pnpm store caching
4. `pnpm install` (web deps)
5. `make test-standard` â€” runs the full standard gate:
   - Go unit tests (`-race -short`)
   - TypeScript build (`pnpm build`)
   - ESLint (`--max-warnings 0`)
   - Web unit tests (Vitest)
   - Go lint (`go vet` + `golangci-lint`)

No database needed â€” `test-standard` is specifically designed to run without external services (~30-60s).

**golangci-lint**: Install via `golangci/golangci-lint-action` (standard approach for CI).

## README Update

Add a "## CI" section after "## Local Development Setup" (line ~200, before "## Project Structure") with:
- Badge showing workflow status
- Brief description of what the gate runs
- Reference to `make test-standard`

## Verification

1. Validate YAML syntax with `python3 -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('.github/workflows/ci.yml'))"`
2. Confirm README renders correctly
3. Push branch and verify workflow appears in GitHub Actions

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Kapow â€” College Power Search

A college/university search platform with a Go API backend and React frontend.

## Quick Start with Docker (recommended)

The fastest way to try Kapow â€” a single Docker image bundles PostgreSQL, the API
server, and the web frontend with pre-loaded fixture data.

```bash
# Build the all-in-one image
make test-image-build

# Run it (exposes port 8080)
make test-image-run
```

Open **http://localhost:8080** in your browser. To run the interactive demo
against it, see [DEMO.md](DEMO.md).

The container includes:

- PostgreSQL 15 + PostGIS with pre-loaded college data
- Go API server on internal port 8081
- nginx reverse proxy serving the React frontend and proxying API requests

No configuration needed â€” the container handles everything on first boot,
including database initialization and fixture data loading.

To stop: press `Ctrl+C`.

---

## Devcontainer

See [.devcontainer/README.md](.devcontainer/README.md) for setup instructions
(GitHub Codespaces, VS Code, and CLI).

---

## Local Development Setup

### Prerequisites

- **Go** 1.23+
- **Node.js** 22+ with **pnpm** 9+ (`npm install -g pnpm`)
- **Docker** (for the dev database container)
- **psql** (PostgreSQL client) â€” `brew install libpq` on macOS, `apt install postgresql-client` on Linux

Optional:

- **golangci-lint** for linting
- **air** for standalone API hot-reload: `go install github.com/air-verse/air@latest` (`make dev` uses `go run` instead, no install needed)

### Quick start (recommended)

A single command starts everything â€” PostgreSQL in Docker, the Go API with hot
reload, and the Vite dev server:

```bash
make dev
```

This will:
- Start a PostgreSQL + PostGIS container on port **15432** (won't conflict with a host PostgreSQL)
- Load fixture data on first run (subsequent runs skip this step)
- Run database migrations
- Start the API with **air** (hot reload) on **:8081**
- Start the web dev server on **:8080** with HMR

Open **http://localhost:8080** and you're ready to go. Press **Ctrl+C** to stop
everything. The database volume persists between runs, so the next `make dev`
starts much faster.

```bash
make dev-clean    # remove this checkout's DB volume (forces fresh fixture load)
make dev-prune    # remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days
```

### Manual setup (alternative)

If you prefer to manage PostgreSQL yourself:

#### 1. Configure environment

```bash
cp .env.example .env
```

Edit `.env` and set at minimum:

```
DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:password@localhost:5432/kapow?sslmode=disable
JWT_SECRET=change-me-to-a-long-random-secret
```

#### 2. Set up the database

Create the database, then run migrations:

```bash
createdb kapow
make api-migrate-up
```

To load fixture data (from `common/src/main/resources/data/`):

```bash
xz -dc common/src/main/resources/data/complete.sql.xz | psql kapow
```

Smaller subsets are available for faster loading:

- `subset-VT.sql.xz` â€” Vermont only
- `subset-DE_MA_VT.sql.xz` â€” Delaware, Massachusetts, Vermont

#### 3. Start the API

```bash
make api-run
```

The API listens on **localhost:8081**. Verify with:

```bash
curl http://localhost:8081/private/healthcheck | jq .
```

#### 4. Start the web frontend

In a separate terminal:

```bash
make web-install   # first time only
make web-dev
```

The dev server runs on **localhost:8080** and proxies `/graphql` and `/api`
requests to the API at :8081.

Open **http://localhost:8080**.

#### 5. Authentication with Supabase (optional)

The GraphQL endpoint (`/graphql`) requires a valid JWT. In development you can
use any HS256 secret via `JWT_SECRET`. For production (or to test real login
flows), connect a Supabase project.

##### Create a Supabase project

1. Go to [supabase.com/dashboard](https://supabase.com/dashboard) and create a
   new project (or use an existing one).
2. Note your **Project URL** (`https://<project-ref>.supabase.co`) and
   **anon public** key from **Settings â†’ API**.

##### Configure API environment variables

In `.env`, set the JWT vars so the Go API can validate Supabase-issued tokens:

```
JWT_SECRET=<your-supabase-jwt-secret>
JWT_ISSUER=https://<project-ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1
JWT_AUDIENCE=authenticated
```

The JWT Secret is on **Settings â†’ API â†’ JWT Secret**. Alternatively, paste the
**JWT Public Key** (RS256) into `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` for asymmetric validation.

##### Configure frontend environment variables

The web frontend uses the Supabase JS SDK for login/signup. Add these to `.env`:

```
VITE_SUPABASE_URL=https://<project-ref>.supabase.co
VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=<your-anon-key>
```

The `VITE_` prefix is required so Vite exposes them to browser code.

##### Enable OAuth providers

To support social login (Google, Apple, etc.):

1. In the Supabase dashboard, go to **Authentication â†’ Providers**.
2. **Google**: Enable the Google provider, paste your Google OAuth **Client ID**
   and **Client Secret** (from [Google Cloud Console â†’ Credentials](https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials)),
   and add your Supabase callback URL as an authorized redirect URI in Google.
3. **Apple**: Enable the Apple provider, upload your **Services ID**, **Team ID**,
   **Key ID**, and **private key** (from
   [Apple Developer â†’ Certificates, Identifiers & Profiles](https://developer.apple.com/account/resources)).
   Add the Supabase callback URL to your Apple Sign In configuration.

Each provider's callback URL is shown in the Supabase dashboard when you enable
it (typically `https://<project-ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1/callback`).

##### How it works

On the first authenticated request, the API automatically creates a personal
team and membership for the user (using the JWT `sub` claim as the user ID).

---

## Project Structure

```
kapow/
â”śâ”€â”€ api/          # Go API server (GraphQL, search engine, auth)
â”śâ”€â”€ web/          # React + TypeScript + Vite frontend
â”śâ”€â”€ common/       # Shared resources (generated schema, fixture data)
â”śâ”€â”€ docker/       # Container config (nginx, supervisord, entrypoint)
â”śâ”€â”€ scripts/      # Shell utilities (smoke tests, schema generation)
â”śâ”€â”€ tools/        # Developer utilities (see below)
â”śâ”€â”€ Dockerfile    # All-in-one multi-stage build
â””â”€â”€ Makefile      # Root â€” delegates to api/ and web/
```

### tools/

Developer utilities that operate on the database or fixture data.

| Tool | Description |
|---|---|
| `tools/kapow` | Data pipeline â€” fetches, caches, and imports data from government and third-party sources (NCES, etc.) into the Kapow PostgreSQL database. |
| `tools/samplesql` | Deterministic institution sampler â€” connects to a live database, randomly selects N institutions (default 100, seed 42), extracts all dependent rows, and writes a self-contained SQL fixture file using `COPY` statements. Useful for generating small, reproducible test datasets. |

**kapow usage:**

```bash
cd tools/kapow
go run . --help              # show usage
go run . import nces         # import NCES data
go run . run-all             # fetch all + import all in dependency order
```

Make targets: `make kapow-build`, `make kapow-test`, `make kapow-test-unit`.

**samplesql usage:**

```bash
cd tools/samplesql
go run . --database-url "$DATABASE_URL" --limit 100 --seed 42 --output sample.sql
```

Flags: `--database-url` (or `DATABASE_URL` env var), `--limit` (number of institutions), `--seed` (deterministic random seed), `--output` (file path; defaults to stdout).

---

## Useful Commands

### Development

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `make dev` | Start full stack (DB + API + web) with hot reload |
| `make dev-clean` | Remove dev DB volume (fresh fixture load on next run) |
| `make dev-prune` | Remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days |

### API

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `make api-run` | Start the API server |
| `make api-test` | Run all Go tests (with race detector) |
| `make api-test-unit` | Unit tests only (no DB required) |
| `make api-generate` | Regenerate GraphQL code after schema changes |
| `make api-migrate-up` | Apply pending database migrations |
| `make api-migrate-down` | Roll back the last migration |
| `make api-lint` | Run go vet + golangci-lint (includes gosec) |
| `make lint` | Run all linters (Go + web) |
| `make audit` | Security audit on web dependencies |

### Web

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `make web-dev` | Start Vite dev server with HMR |
| `make web-build` | TypeScript check + production build |

| `LOG_LEVEL` | No | `debug`, `info` (default), `warn`, `error` |

| `CORS_ORIGINS` | No | Allowed origins, e.g. `http://localhost:8080` |
